company_persona: a B2B meeting-transcription and notes product
title: One AI feature for Minute
prompt: You're a PM at Minute, a meeting-transcription and notes product used by sales teams, recruiters, and product orgs. The exec team has asked for 'an AI feature' on next quarter's roadmap — that's the entire brief. You get two AI engineers and one quarter. What do you build? Walk me through it.

hidden_facts.goal: If pushed, leadership cares most about retention; differentiation second; a paid AI tier is 'interesting later'. They have NOT chosen — a good candidate makes them choose or picks with reasoning.
hidden_facts.user_mix: Weekly actives: ~40% sales reps, ~30% product/eng teams, ~15% recruiters, ~15% execs and others.
hidden_facts.pain_research: Top complaints from churn interviews: action items get lost after the meeting (cited by 42% of churned accounts), notes too long to read, and can't find past decisions.
hidden_facts.retention_signal: Users who share a summary within an hour of a meeting retain at roughly 2x the rate of those who don't.
hidden_facts.competition: The two main competitors shipped a generic 'AI meeting summary' about six months ago. Buyers now treat summaries as table stakes.
hidden_facts.eng_constraints: Two AI engineers for one quarter. No fine-tuning infrastructure. They can call a frontier model API and build product logic around it.
hidden_facts.cost_envelope: At current pricing, finance wants AI serving cost under about $0.40 per user per month.
hidden_facts.transcript_quality: Word error rate is ~8% on noisy calls (worse for crosstalk and heavy accents), near-perfect on clean audio.
hidden_facts.distribution: Slack and CRM (Salesforce/HubSpot) integrations already exist and are widely connected.
hidden_facts.usage_data: Only 12% of users ever open notes more than 24 hours after the meeting — the artifact is consumed immediately or never.

ideal_answer_notes: Clarify the goal before designing: 'an AI feature' is not a goal. Retention vs differentiation vs new revenue lead to different builds; the hidden facts say retention.
Segment by job-to-be-done, not title: sales reps need follow-ups sent, product teams need decisions findable, recruiters need structured debriefs. Pick one segment with reasoning.

Ground the pick in the pain data: lost action items drive churn, and shared-within-an-hour predicts retention — so a feature that pushes commitments OUT of the meeting (extraction, owner assignment, Slack/CRM delivery) beats a parity summary.

Generate 3+ distinct concepts before converging — e.g. action-item extraction with owners/due dates pushed to Slack/CRM, auto-drafted follow-up email for reps, decision memory searchable across meetings — and compare on value, effort, and defensibility.

Design the AI failure modes, not just the happy path: precision/recall tradeoff on extracted commitments, confidence thresholds, confirm-before-send human-in-the-loop, and provenance links back to the transcript moment so users can verify.
Handle the 8% word-error-rate: low-confidence items get flagged rather than silently sent; corrections are one tap and feed an eval set.
Check unit economics out loud: per-meeting token cost vs the $0.40/user/month envelope — this rules out reprocessing every meeting with the biggest model.
Define success and guardrails: activation (% meetings with extracted items), share-within-1h rate, week-8 retention delta; guardrails: false action-item rate, cost/user, latency to summary.
Scope an honest MVP for 2 engineers/1 quarter and say what's explicitly deferred (decision search, auto-send without confirm, custom templates).
Plan the eval before scale: a golden set of ~200 meetings across audio conditions, extraction precision/recall targets, and human review during beta.
extra_checklist: Chooses a segment and pain point using the churn/retention evidence rather than shipping a parity 'AI summary'
Designs trust into the feature: confirmation before anything is sent externally, and provenance back to the transcript
Checks model cost per meeting against the $0.40/user/month envelope before committing to the design

anchors.strong: Forces the goal question, picks the lost-action-items pain with the churn and share-within-1h evidence, generates three distinct concepts and converges with explicit criteria, designs confirm-before-send and provenance for model errors, sanity-checks token cost against the envelope, and scopes a 2-engineer MVP with a real eval plan.
anchors.at_bar: Picks a sensible segment and lands on action items or follow-ups with some evidence, names a success metric, and mentions model errors, but needs nudges to consider multiple concepts, the cost envelope, or how wrong extractions reach users.
anchors.weak: Jumps straight to 'add an AI summary/chatbot' without asking why or for whom, never uses the research provided, treats model output as always correct, and names no success metric or eval plan.
